coredata.dc:title: A universal facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots from cellulose-based biowaste for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ ions and intracellular bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Chaoge
Xing, Malcolm
Wu, Qilin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), especially heteroatom doped CDs derived from natural resources, have attracted growing interest due to their multiple potential applications. In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N/S-CDs) with a high quantum yield of 13.3% were synthesized by a facile approach based on one-step combustion treatment of cellulose-based biowaste of willow catkin. Due to the existence of urea and sulfuric acid used in the combustion process, the generated yield of the obtained N/S-CDs was raised to be as high as 14.3%. N/S-CDs were characterized by high-resolution TEM, FT-IR spectroscopy, XPS spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. The formation mechanism of N/S-CDs was also explored. These N/S-CDs with an average diameter of 7.3 nm demonstrated superior photo-stability, low cytotoxicity, pH stability and could be used for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, based on their favorable feature of excellent biocompatibility and Fe3+ ions detection capability, the use of such N/S-CDs for intracellular Fe3+ ions detection application was also demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots
Cellulose-based biowaste
Forming mechanism
Intracellular bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The role of sulfuric acid and urea on the synthesis of high yield N/S-CDs
Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions based on N/S-CDs
Cytotoxicity and intracellular Fe3+ ions detection of N/S-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The SEM images of willow catkin; TEM images of the (b) CDs and (c–d) N/S-CDs (inset: particle size distribution).
Structure characterization of N/S-CDs. (a) FT-IR spectra; (b) XPS survey spectrum; (c) C1S spectra; (d) O1S spectra; (e) N1S spectra and (f) S2P spectra of the resulting N/S-CDs.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption, maximum PL excitation and emission spectra of N/S-CDs (inset: optical image of N/S-CDs aqueous solution under white light (left) and UV lamp at 365 nm (right)). (b) PL emission spectra of N/S-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) PL life time data of N/S-CDs. Effects of (d) pH value (inset: Zeta potentials of the N/S-CDs within the range of pH 1–12), (e) UV irradiation time and (f) storage time on PL intensity of N/S-CDs (inset: the N/S-CDs aqueous solution after 8 months of storage).

(a) PL intensity ratios (F/F0) of the N/S-CDs in the presence of various metal ions (1 mM; black bars) and the subsequent addition of Fe3+ ions (1 mM; red bars). (b) PL spectra of N/S-CDs aqueous solution in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ ions. (c) The fluorescence quenching photographs of N/S-CDs with different Fe3+ ions concentrations (from left to right: 0, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 and 600 μM). (d) The dependence of F/F0 on the concentration of Fe3+ ions within the range of 20–900 μM (inset: the linear relationship of F/F0 versus the concentration of Fe3+ ions over the range of 40–700 μM). The error bars represent standard deviations based on five independent measurements. (pH 7.0，0.03 M PBS，λex = 310 nm, C [N/S-CDs] = 0.45 mg/mL). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Cell viability assays of HeLa cells after 24 h treatment with different concentrations of N/S-CDs. (b) the bright and (c–f) dark field CLSM images of HeLa cells labeled with N/S-CDs (40 μg/mL) under different conditions: (c) only N/S-CDs, (d) N/S-CDs + 10 μM Fe3+, (e) N/S-CDs + 20 μM Fe3+, and (f) N/S-CDs + 40 μM Fe3+ (inset: schematic illustration of fluorescence quenching of N/S-CDs with Fe3+ ions).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of N/S-CDs from cellulose-based biowaste willow catkin.
Proposed mechanism for the conversion of N/S-Cellulose to N/S-CDs.
Comparison of CDs from willow catkin and other natural resources.
Comparison of the fluorescent probe based on CDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions.

row.entry: Nitrogen doped CDs derived from Phyllanthus acidus

head.ce:title: A universal facile synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots from cellulose-based biowaste for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ ions and intracellular bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Manitoba R3T 5V6, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), especially heteroatom doped CDs derived from natural resources, have attracted growing interest due to their multiple potential applications. In this work, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N/S-CDs) with a high quantum yield of 13.3% were synthesized by a facile approach based on one-step combustion treatment of cellulose-based biowaste of willow catkin. Due to the existence of urea and sulfuric acid used in the combustion process, the generated yield of the obtained N/S-CDs was raised to be as high as 14.3%. N/S-CDs were characterized by high-resolution TEM, FT-IR spectroscopy, XPS spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy. The formation mechanism of N/S-CDs was also explored. These N/S-CDs with an average diameter of 7.3 nm demonstrated superior photo-stability, low cytotoxicity, pH stability and could be used for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions. Furthermore, based on their favorable feature of excellent biocompatibility and Fe3+ ions detection capability, the use of such N/S-CDs for intracellular Fe3+ ions detection application was also demonstrated.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a recent member of carbon-based photoluminescent nanomaterials, have emerged as the most attractive alternative to the conventional organic fluorescent dyes because of their unique optical properties [1–3]. Over the past decade, reports on the preparation of CDs are gradually mature, and researchers have increasingly focused on the simple and cost-effective preparation of CDs. Among them, CDs derived from green and inexpensive natural precursors, also known as biomass based CDs, have recently attracted extensive attention for their extraordinary properties, including favorable photoluminescence (PL) properties, low-cytotoxicity, superior biocompatibility, especially the abundant and inexpensive nature compared to the conventional CDs and organic fluorescent dyes [4–6].

After progressing this far, a wide variety of waste biomass materials, including glycine [7], food waste [1], willow leaves [8], and shiitake mushroom [9] etc., have been utilized as carbon precursors to prepare CDs by using various techniques such as thermal oxidation, electrochemical oxidation, ultrasound irradiation, and hydrothermal treatment. Nevertheless, each of these reported techniques has its own limitations like not definitely useless raw resources, complicated synthesis conditions, time and energy consumption, or need for expensive instruments. And, to the best of our knowledge, currently available techniques are difficult in fabricating biowaste based CDs with both high quantum yield (QY) and generated yield (GY), which hinders their wide applications in environmental, biological and life sciences. Hence, it is highly demanded to explore a facile and quick approach for the scalable synthesis of high-quality CDs from easily available and low-cost carbon source.

As it's known, biomass cellulose is the most abundant organic polymer on the Earth. Therefore, transform cellulose-based biowaste to high value-added CDs is of great significance. Incorporation of heteroatoms into CDs can tailor their electron-donor properties and consequently change the electron transition gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), thereby enhancing their capability of PL and endow them with specific functions [10,11]. Particularly, compared with single heteroatom doping that improves merely one aspect of properties, multielement doping can enhance overall performances of the materials due to the synergetic effect [12]. However, cellulose is insoluble in water and most organic compounds due to its tightly packed crystalline structure, which make it difficult to obtain heteroatom doped CDs from cellulose [13–15]. Thus, developing simple and fast methods suitable for synthesizing heteroatom doped carbon nanomaterials from cellulose is still a great challenge.

As a kind of common cellulose-based biowaste, willow catkin is the flower of willow trees, and exist in the form of hollow and thin-walled fiber (Fig. 1a), which is most effective to be infiltrated and burnt. Herein, we developed a facile and quick approach to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs) with both high QY (13.3%) and GY (14.3%) via one-step combustion treatment of willow catkin. The prepared N/S-CDs demonstrated excellent photo-stability and low cytotoxicity, which made this material a suitable candidate for biomedical applications. Meanwhile, the resulting N/S-CDs could serve as a very effective intracellular probe for selective detection of Fe3+ ions. This work not only offers a novel way for the construction of N/S-CDs using willow catkin, but also enables us to take advantages of flammable cellulose-based biowaste to design and prepare multielement doped CDs in the future research.

Cellulose-based biowaste was collected from the campus of Donghua University (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid, urea, ethanol, PbCl2, BaCl2, CaCl2, FeCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, FeCl3, AlCl3, MgCl2, HgCl2, NiCl2 and CrCl3 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate (99%, suitable for fluorescence) and other chemical reagents were purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water was also used in the experiments.

The N/S-CDs were efficiently fabricated by a one-step combustion treatment of cellulose-based biowaste willow catkin (Scheme 1 ). Typically, the willow catkin (5 g) were soaked in 1000 mL N/S aqueous solution containing 12 g of urea and 35 mL of sulfuric acid (98%) at room temperature for 1 h. After that, the soaked willow catkin was dried in an oven at 80 °C and then completely burned to ashes in the air. The ashes were then ground into powder with ceramic mortar and dispersed in 1 L ultrapure water. After being fully dispersed, the black solution was further purified by filtration through a 0.22 μm mixed cellulose membrane filter, and then dialyzed with dialysis bag (MWCO = 500) to remove large particles and other small molecular compounds. In the end, the powder of N/S-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying for further research. For comparison, under the same other conditions, we also prepared CDs from the cellulose-based biowaste that with no pretreatment, pretreated by single urea solution or sulfuric acid solution, named as CDs, N-CDs and S-CDs, respectively.

To investigate the morphology and composition of the N/S-CDs, the obtained N/S-CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR spectroscopy, XPS spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy, respectively. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1c), the as-prepared N/S-CDs were found to be roughly spherical particles with an average diameter of 7.3 nm, and mono-dispersing in aqueous media. The lattice spacing distance of resulting N/S-CDs can be identified from the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1d), which was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100 facet) [16]. This result indicated the crystalline behavior of N/S-CDs. Unlike N/S-CDs, the CDs obtained without N/S aqueous solution pretreatment did not show clear morphology and crystalline structure (Fig. 1b). The typical Raman spectrum of the resulting N/S-CDs (Fig. S1) demonstrated two distinctive peaks, which consistent with the HRTEM image and further support the fact that the as-prepared N/S-CDs exhibited graphite lattice spacing nature [17].

FT-IR measurements were conducted to gain further structural insight into CDs and N/S-CDs (Fig. 2a). The broad bands appeared at 3410 cm−1 for both CDs and N/S-CDs were corresponding to OH/NH stretching vibrations, and the characteristic band at 1635 cm−1 was assigned to CC vibration [18]. Compared to CDs, N/S-CDs showed the predominant peak of CN bending vibration at 1390 cm−1. Although no obvious characteristic bands on CS or SOx groups were found in the FT-IR spectrum of N/S-CDs, XPS spectroscopy was further applied to confirm the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur into the structure of N/S-CDs (Fig. 2b). The results revealed that the N/S-CDs mainly consist of oxygen (O1S, 532 eV), carbon (C1S, 285 eV), nitrogen (N1S, 399.4 eV) and sulfur (S2p, 167 eV) elements [19,20]. Unlike CDs contained only a small amount of nitrogen and no sulfur element (Fig. S2), the relative contents of C, O, N and S on the surface of N/S-CDs were 32.47%, 44.84%, 15.55% and 7.14%, respectively.

In addition, the high-resolution XPS was demonstrated to speculate the possible combination modes of N, S, C, and O in N/S-CDs. In detail, the three peaks of C1S could attributed to CC (284.5 eV), C–N/C-S (285.5 eV), and CO (288.2 eV) bands, respectively (Fig. 2c) [21]. While the O1S spectrum in Fig. 2d exhibited two peaks at 531.3 and 532.4 eV, attributed to CO and CO groups, respectively [4]. The N1S spectrum in Fig. 2e showed two peaks at 400.2 and 401.7 eV, corresponded to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively. The S2p spectrum in Fig. 2f displayed two different types of sulfur atoms: C-SO3 with a binding energy at 168.5 eV and C-SO4 at 169.3 eV [22]. The XPS results showed satisfactory agreement with FT-IR investigation, clearly indicating that N and S atoms had been successfully doped into the prepared N/S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of sulfuric acid and urea on the synthesis of high yield N/S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been previously reported that both N- and S-doping might be responsible for the efficient radiative recombination and then improve the QY of CDs significantly [19,23]. The specific QY and GY data comparison for all four types of carbon dots (CDs, N-CDs, S-CDs and N/S-CDs) were compared in Table S1. Compared to the controlled CDs, QY of N-CDs increased, whereas GY of S-CDs increased as well. But only when sulfuric acid and urea are combined can high performance N/S-CDs with both high QY and GY be achieved.Sulfuric acid can promote dehydration, while the flame retardant effect of urea can prevent the consumption of carbon. The combination of the two components can effectively improve the yield. The possible mechanism for the successful preparation of N/S-CDs will be discussed in detail later.

Compared with CDs from other natural precursors using various synthetic routes, the QY and GY results of our N/S-CDs are relatively higher (Table 1 ). Most importantly, in addition to willow catkin, the N/S aqueous solution can also be applied to many cellulose-based biowaste for the scalable synthesis of high quality CDs (Fig. S3). Hence, the one-step combustion approach reported in the present study is competitive for large-scale production of N/S-CDs from cellulose-based biowaste.

As stated above, the N/S-CDs exhibited both higher QY and GY than most of other biomass-based CDs, which may be due to the important role of sulfuric acid and urea systems played in cellulose combustion process. Although the mechanism of cellulose combustion process is rather complex [29], based on the structural and thermal stability analysis of N/S-Cellulose, we put forward a possible proposal to elucidate the main regularities and mechanism in the N/S-Cellulose combustion process (Scheme 2 ). Different from the one-step reaction of the pyrolysis of pure cellulose, the N/S-cellulose – N/S-CDs transition, according to the TGA curves (inset in Scheme 2), is reasonable to be subdivided into four main stages: dehydration (50–190 °C), nucleophilic/electrophilic addition (190–220 °C), ring-opened reaction (220–260 °C) and carbon hexagons formation (above 260 °C):

The sulfuric acid, at the initial stages of N/S-Cellulose combustion process, is considered to work as dehydration catalyst, thus sharply accelerating dehydration of cellulose units and suppressing the release of volatile organic substance (methanol, acetic acid and tar), the result of which is higher GY of carbon than that of pure cellulose (TG curves in Scheme 2) [30,31].

Although the cleavage process of intermolecular ether bands is rather complex, 5 predominant types of aromatic intermediate structures are proposed as shown in Scheme 2. The first one is the common intermediate structure without sulfur acid and urea participated in, and this structure has been confirmed according to the previous reports [32]. The second and third intermediate structures containing nitrogen formed through the nucleophilic addition reaction of urea to CO bands. Simultaneously, by electrophilic addition reaction of sulfuric acid with CC bands (④,⑤ in Scheme 2), two different types of intermediate structures containing sulfur formed.

The major part of thermal degradation occurs in the ring-opened process. In this process, the thermal cleavages of unreacted CO bands, CO bands and some CC bands yield large amounts of volatile products (H2O, CO and CO2), which leads to the final breakdown of each cellulose ring unit, and then results in the formation of intermediate structure fragments [32].

The intermediate structural fragments of cellulose decomposition represent the “building blocks” for the repolymerization process to form carbon hexagons structure and, ultimately, hexagonal carbon layer [33]. The nanoscale nitrogen and sulfur co-doped hexagonal carbon layer, we can call it N/S-CDs, eventually generated under the intense combustion process. In conclusion, sulfuric acid and urea play a crucial role in the process of cellulose – N/S-CDs transition, leading to the reduction of byproducts, the increased QY and GY, and eventually the incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur into the structure of N/S-CDs.

The PL properties of the resulting N/S-CDs were then investigated. As shown in Fig. 3a, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibited a peak in the UV region at around 280 nm, which was ascribed to the typical absorption of the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [34,35]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelength of N/S-CDs was around 310 nm and 370 nm, respectively. The inset in Fig. 3a showed that the aqueous solution of N/S-CDs exhibited intense blue fluorescence under 360 nm wavelength UV light. Additionally, the N/S-CDs displayed excitation-dependent emission behavior (Fig. 3b), that was, the maximum emission peak shifted to a longer wavelength and the PL intensity decreased when the excitation wavelength increased from 310 to 420 nm. The excitation-dependent emission behavior is a general feature of fluorescent carbon materials. This behavior may be ascribed to the various emission centers of N/S-CDs with different sizes and different surface states [19,35,36]. To confirm this, we measured the PL decay of the N/S-CDs and found that it was non-monoexponential with an average lifetime of 5.94 ns (Fig. 3c).

Significantly, the resulting N/S-CDs were found to possess excellent photo-stability under different conditions. We used NaOH and HCl to adjust the pH values of N/S-CDs aqueous solution to the range of 1 to 13. As shown in Fig. 3d, the PL intensity of N/S-CDs aqueous solutions was strong and even stable in a wide range of pH values from 3 to 11 except for the strong acidity (pH = 1) and alkaline (pH = 13) environments, probably because the electronic transition of some functional groups would be disrupted in the present of too many H+ or OH– ions. Likewise, the PL intensity decreased less than 10% after UV irradiation at 360 nm for 6 h (Fig. 3e). The pH-stability and photobleaching- resistant feature of the N/S-CDs can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsions among the negatively charged N/S-CDs nanoparticles (inset in Fig. 3d) [34]. In addition, as revealed in Figs. S4 and 3f, the PL intensity of N/S-CDs showed almost no obvious variation (less than 10%) with the varying concentrations of NaCl (0 to 6 M) and long-time storage (8 months) at 4 °C. The superior PL stability of the resulting N/S-CDs will be beneficial to their applications in sensing and bioimaging [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions based on N/S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that Fe3+ ion is an essential element of heme group and plays crucial roles in oxygen metabolism. However, both its deficiency and excessive from the normal permissible limit may disturb the balance of cellular processes and may cause severe diseases such as organ failure, inflammation, Alzheimer's disease, and eventually death [38]. Therefore, the development of effective sensing system for selective and sensitive analysis of Fe3+ ion is of great significance for clinical applications.

To assess the selectivity of the obtained N/S-CDs to Fe3+ ions, the PL intensity ratio F/F0 (where F0 and F are PL intensity of N/S-CDs aqueous solution in the absence and presence of different metal ions, respectively) of the N/S-CDs (0.45 mg/mL) at 370 nm was analyzed upon adding various metal ions. As demonstrated in Fig. 4a, the PL of N/S-CDs exhibited highly selective quenching to Fe3+ ions (90% quenching upon addition of 1 mM Fe3+), whereas the influences of other metal ions including Fe2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cr2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Pb2+ and Ni2+ on PL properties of N/S-CDs were almost negligible. Furthermore, a significant quenching was observed after the extra addition of Fe3+ ions in the mixed solution (Fig. 4a, red bars), implying the outstanding selectivity and credible anti-interference ability of the N/S-CDs sensing system for Fe3+ ions detection. Previous studies have shown that the large amounts of oxygen functional groups and S atoms on the surface of N/S-CDs had a higher affinity for Fe3+ ions in comparison with other metal ions, which was beneficial for the stronger chelation or coordination interactions between Fe3+ ions and N/S-CDs [39–41]. The above stable N/S-CDs-Fe3+ binding could probably be responsible for the good quenching selectivity performance of N/S-CDs [42,43].

Dependence of PL quenching effect on Fe3+ ion concentration was further investigated. It can be clearly seen in Fig.4b that the PL intensity of N/S-CDs sensing system gradually decreased with the progressively increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions, and the PL emission of N/S-CDs aqueous solution correspondingly changed from bright blue to colorless under UV irradiation (Fig.4c). More importantly, as shown in Fig. 4d, there was a good linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio F/F0 and the Fe3+ ions concentration within the range of 40–700 μM, and the detection limit was estimated to be 0.03 μM based on a 3δ/S (where δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear response region) [44,45], which was much lower than recently published assays based on CDs from different precursors (Table 2 ). Therefore, the N/S-CDs could potentially be used as a sensor for Fe3+ ions detection.

The possible explanation for the strong PL quenching effect of N/S-CDs-Fe3+ system may be relevant to the electron transfer mechanism (inset in Fig. 5 ) [20]. Initially, the free N/S-CDs exhibited strong PL properties due to the completely electron transferring from LUMO to HOMO, known as radiative electron/hole recombination (inset in Fig. 5c). After the addition of Fe3+ ions, the electrons in the LUMO state of N/S-CDs would transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions, which further facilitated non-radiative electron/hole recombination and resulted in the consequently quenching of PL (inset in Fig. 5f) [50]. In order to gain insight into possible PL quenching mechanisms, the PL lifetime investigation of N/S-CDs in the absence and the presence of Fe3+ ions were carried out [51]. As depicted in Fig. S5, before and after addition of Fe3+ ions, the average PL lifetime of N/S-CDs was decreased from 5.94 ns to 4.76 ns. This result indicated the occurrence of dynamic quenching between N/S-CDs and Fe3+ ions and confirmed the electron transfer mechanism mentioned above [43,50,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and intracellular Fe3+ ions detection of N/S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Benefited from their high fluorescence QY, excellent photostability in the biological pH environment and resistance to the high ionic strength, the as-obtained N/S-CDs were further applied for bioimaging applications. The inherent cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of the N/S-CDs towards HeLa cells (a human cervical cancer cell line) was evaluated through standard CCK assays. As depicted in Fig. 5a, it is clearly revealed that the N/S-CDs have not imposed any significant toxicity (less than 10%) to HeLa cells even with a high concentration of 400 μg/mL and long incubation period (24 h), highlighting the less cytotoxicity and better biocompatibility of the resulting N/S-CDs than many other reported semiconductor quantum dots and CDs synthesized from chemicals [5,53,54]. Therefore, our N/S-CDs derived from cellulose-based biowaste can be potentially safe for in vitro imaging.

To the best of our knowledge, there are few studies on the intracellular Fe3+ ions detection using biowaste-based CDs. Herein, we further evaluated the capability of N/S-CDs in intracellular Fe3+ ions detection. As shown in the confocal microscope images (Fig. 5 b–c), after being incubated with 40 μg/mL of the N/S-CDs for 24 h, the HeLa cells showed obvious blue photoluminescence in the areas of the cell membrane and cytoplasm under 405 nm laser excitation wavelength. The results revealed that the N/S-CDs can enter into cells [55]. Moreover, The fluorescence intensity decreased distinctly upon the addition of Fe3+ ions, and there was no obvious blue emission detected after the cells were treated with 40 μM Fe3+ ions (Fig. 5d–f), suggesting that the fluorescence intensity of N/S-CDs is related to the concentration of Fe3+ and capable of labeling the Fe3+ ions in living cells.

We have developed a quick and efficient approach to synthesis nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs with cellulose-based biowaste. The roles of sulfuric acid and urea system in the synthesis procedure are not only to improve both the quantum yield (13.3%) and generated yield (14.3%), but also to endow N/S-CDs with the capability for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions. The fabricated N/S-CDs with an average diameter of 7.3 nm exhibited excellent photo stability, low cytotoxicity and were successfully employed for in vitro imaging and Fe3+ ions detection in HeLa cells. The proposed method provides a facile avenue for the production of cellulose-based CDs for various applications in biological detection and clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by National Key R&D Program of China (2016YFB0303201) & the Research and Innovation Project of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (No. 14zz069). M.X. would like to thank the Discovery Grant of National Science and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) and NSERC Discovery Accelerator Supplement Award. Authors appreciate Prof. Xu Yongfen for her beneficial discussion on the co-doping mechanism.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental details and extensive graphs are available in the online version of the paper. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.02.003.

Efficient synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots by microreactor method and their application in Fe3+ ion detection
Facile synthesis of fluorescence carbon dots from sweet potato for Fe3+ sensing and cell imaging

A green synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from itaconic acid and their application as an efficient sensor for Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium
Facile synthesis of N-rich carbon quantum dots from porphyrins as efficient probes for bioimaging and biosensing in living cells
